current_fact.fact_text: On 23 March 1983, U.S. President Ronald Reagan announced the Strategic Defense Initiative, a plan to build space‑ and ground‑based systems to intercept incoming nuclear missiles. Nicknamed 'Star Wars', it blended genuine research into missile defense with almost science‑fiction visions of lasers, particle beams, and orbiting battle stations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

current_fact.match_reason: A futuristic missile shield dubbed 'Star Wars' fits the bright, machine‑driven, cosmic dance energy of an episode about electronic evolution and spacey optimism.

queue.fact_text: In 1991, the Revolutionary United Front (RUF), backed by Charles Taylor’s National Patriotic Front of Liberia, crossed into Sierra Leone and ignited an 11‑year civil war. The conflict would become notorious for 'blood diamonds', child soldiers, and brutal amputations, eventually reshaping global debates about resource exploitation and war crimes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A West African conflict over power and resources contrasts with an episode celebrating West African musical resistance and political consciousness through Afrobeat and highlife.

queue.fact_text: On 23 March 1994, an F‑16 fighter collided with a C‑130 transport at Pope Air Force Base in North Carolina, sending flaming debris into a group of paratroopers preparing to jump. The tragedy, known as the Green Ramp disaster, killed 24 soldiers on the ground and became a somber case study in aviation safety and risk on crowded military airfields.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The sudden, catastrophic collision of high‑energy machines echoes in an 'Electric Shock' episode filled with blazing guitars and kinetic charge—sonic turbulence to match aerial chaos.

queue.fact_text: In 2001, Russia deorbited its aging Mir space station, but two years later on 23 March 2003, attention turned back to Earth as the Battle of Nasiriyah became one of the first major engagements of the Iraq invasion. A confused urban fight around bridges and an ambushed U.S. convoy highlighted how quickly modern war can turn chaotic once boots leave the highway.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The murky, cinematic atmosphere of urban combat in Nasiriyah pairs with a trip‑hop episode whose soundtracks often mirror the tension, ambiguity, and slow‑motion unease of modern warfare.

queue.fact_text: On 23 March 2009, FedEx Express Flight 80, a McDonnell Douglas MD‑11 cargo jet, crashed while landing in strong gusty winds at Tokyo Narita Airport. The aircraft bounced, rolled inverted, and exploded, a stark reminder that even in the age of long‑haul automation, a few seconds of turbulence near the runway can be fatal.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A crash caused by forces at the edge of human control pairs with an episode about disciplined movement and training—Muay Thai as the deliberate, grounded counter to chaos in motion.

queue.fact_text: In 2014, the World Health Organization reported cases of Ebola in rural southeastern Guinea, marking the start of the largest Ebola outbreak in history. Over the next two years the virus would leap across borders, test global health systems, and teach hard lessons about early detection, community trust, and the speed of an invisible threat.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A global health emergency became a crash course in epidemiology and public trust; an episode about learning, science, and embracing difficulty reflects the steep, painful learning curve of the Ebola crisis.

queue.fact_text: On 23 March 2018, Peru’s President Pedro Pablo Kuczynski resigned amid a sweeping corruption scandal linked to the Odebrecht affair and Operation Car Wash. His fall showed how a Brazilian investigation into construction kickbacks had quietly spun a web across Latin America’s political elites.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A transnational corruption network and a web of digital dependence both explore how invisible systems shape behavior; the episode’s critical look at our connections mirrors scrutiny of political entanglements.

queue.fact_text: On 23 March 2020, Prime Minister Boris Johnson announced the United Kingdom’s first COVID‑19 lockdown, ordering residents to stay home except for essential reasons. Overnight, bustling cities fell eerily quiet, and a modern island nation conducted a vast social experiment in isolation, mutual aid, and reimagined daily life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

queue.match_reason: A London‑centered lockdown reshaped the city’s rhythms; an episode that sonically walks through London becomes a time‑travel postcard to the streets that fell silent in 2020.

queue.fact_text: The War of the Pacific’s first shots at Topáter were fought over desert nitrates, but decades later a different kind of invisible resource—radioactivity—became both promise and threat. From nuclear tests to missile shields, 23 March’s history stitches battlefields, labs, and deserts into one long story of what humans will do for invisible power.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The episode’s title 'November Bomb' and its explosive, genre‑spanning energy echo a long arc of conflicts over unseen resources and power, from nitrates to nukes.

queue.fact_text: In 1977, David Frost’s interviews with Richard Nixon blurred the line between journalism and performance, but by 23 March of that year, the first taping showed how much a carefully crafted conversation could feel like a trial. Millions later watched a former president sweat under stage lights rather than cross‑examination, setting a template for future 'event' interviews.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Nixon’s attempt to reframe his legacy through a media spectacle parallels an episode on 'unboxing' identity and confronting uncomfortable truths, carried by introspective, confrontational hip‑hop.

queue.fact_text: On 23 March 2001, as Mir fell, and in other years tornadoes and wars flared on the same date, a quieter frontier has been shaping culture: sound correction. From fragile tape takes to Auto‑Tune, the human urge to 'fix' imperfections has changed how we remember voices—subtly rewriting what we think real emotion should sound like.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: As the day’s events show technology reshaping war and space, this episode zooms into a subtler tech revolution: Auto‑Tune and the ethics of perfection in sound.

queue.fact_text: Across the 20th and 21st centuries, 23 March has quietly linked explorers, coup leaders, astronauts, archbishops, and epidemiologists. Seen from afar—say, from Sedna—the date looks less like a list of headlines and more like a mixtape of humanity’s extremes: curiosity and conquest, care and catastrophe, each track changing the key of the planet just a little.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A zoomed‑out reflection on all these March 23 stories belongs with a 'Destination Sedna' episode about voyaging through different territories—sonic and historical—as one continuous journey.

published.fact_text: In 1965, NASA’s Gemini 3 mission became the United States’ first two‑person spaceflight, with astronauts Gus Grissom and John Young piloting a tiny capsule that orbited Earth three times in under five hours. Young secretly smuggled a corned‑beef sandwich aboard, making this pioneering orbital test flight also infamous as the mission with the most controversial space snack.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal spaceflight milestone pairs with an episode themed around 'space' and shifting perspectives, echoing the experimental leap of Gemini 3.

published.fact_text: On 23 March 2001, the Russian space station Mir—once the largest human‑made object in orbit and home to dozens of long‑duration missions—was deliberately deorbited after 15 years in space. It broke apart in the atmosphere and splashed into the Pacific Ocean near Fiji, ending an era when Mir symbolized both Soviet ambition and international cooperation in orbit.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: Mir’s slow, controlled fall from orbit feels like a long fade‑out, fitting an introductory 'Evening Flow' that drifts beyond the expected and marks the end of one chapter, start of another.

published.fact_text: In 1806, after reaching the Pacific Ocean, the Lewis and Clark Expedition turned back east to begin its grueling return journey across an almost unmapped North America. Their Corps of Discovery had traversed more than 4,000 miles, and the homeward trek would be just as perilous, filled with tense encounters, near‑starvation, and meticulous note‑taking that reshaped maps of a continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A months‑long, water‑threaded expedition fits with a seafaring episode about shared journeys, resilience, and the rhythm of traveling together.

published.fact_text: On 23 March 1839, a devastating earthquake destroyed the former imperial capital of Inwa (Ava) in what is now Myanmar. The quake toppled centuries‑old monasteries and palace structures in seconds, abruptly erasing much of a dynastic heartland that had dominated Burmese politics for generations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A sudden, violent shaking of the planet contrasts with an episode about slowing down and sensing Earth’s rhythms, offering a gentle, reflective counterpoint to geological upheaval.

published.fact_text: The Battle of Topáter on 23 March 1879 was the opening clash of the War of the Pacific, fought high in the Atacama Desert over nitrate‑rich territories. A small Bolivian‑Peruvian force dug in along a river ford faced modern Chilean troops, foreshadowing how control of desert minerals would shape South American borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A desert battle over resources pairs with desert‑born music about survival, land, and resistance, translating the harsh Atacama front into a different sand‑shaped soundscape.

published.fact_text: In 1909, just weeks after leaving the White House, Theodore Roosevelt departed New York for a year‑long Smithsonian–Roosevelt African expedition. Part scientific mission and part grand hunt, the safari collected thousands of animal specimens for the Smithsonian and National Geographic, while also fixing a dramatic, often problematic, image of Africa in the Western imagination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the Roosevelt safari and the orca dives are nature‑obsessed expeditions at the edge of the wild; the episode reframes that exploratory drive through a modern, conservation‑minded lens.

published.fact_text: On 23 March 1913, a deadly tornado outbreak tore through the central United States even as catastrophic flooding ravaged the Ohio River watershed. With more than 240 people killed by twisters and about 650 dying in the simultaneous floods, the overlapping disasters spurred new thinking about weather forecasting and flood control.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'é Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: An episode about contrasting atmospheres—fog versus volcanic fire—mirrors the collision of air (tornadoes) and water (floods), turning extreme weather into musical mood swings.

published.fact_text: During World War I’s German Spring Offensive, 23 March 1918 saw the 10th Battalion of the Royal West Kent Regiment effectively annihilated in the chaos of Operation Michael. Many survivors were captured as prisoners of war, turning a single day on the Western Front into a defining trauma for a local British regiment whose village memorials still list long rolls of the same surnames.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A devastating wartime loss is paired with an introspective episode about recovery and memory, inviting listeners to look back with awareness at pain, trauma, and healing.

published.fact_text: In 1965, the same year space capsules orbited Earth, an entirely different frontier opened in the living room as color television began its worldwide spread—yet it was the later broadcast of events like the Nixon–Frost interviews in 1977 that revealed TV’s power to interrogate power. When David Frost sat down with Richard Nixon over 12 marathon sessions, one of history’s most choreographed mea culpas unfolded not in a courtroom but on videotape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The Nixon–Frost interviews centered on political accountability and abuse of power, themes echoed fiercely in a politically charged episode dedicated to Rage Against the Machine.

published.fact_text: In 1978, the first United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL) troops arrived to monitor a fragile peace along the Blue Line. Their mission—to stand, unarmed or lightly armed, between militias, armies, and civilians—turned peacekeeping itself into a kind of quiet experiment in shared security.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: Peacekeeping is a kind of diplomatic travel and perspective shift; the episode’s theme of movement, new places, and quiet change resonates with UN troops navigating complex foreign terrain to hold a fragile calm.

published.fact_text: On 23 March 1980, Archbishop Óscar Romero of El Salvador used his final homily to implore soldiers to lay down their guns and stop killing fellow Salvadorans. Broadcast over the radio, his words—“In the name of God, and in the name of this suffering people, I beg you, I beseech you, I order you…”—turned a sermon into a direct, life‑or‑death appeal for conscience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Romero’s moral cry against violence aligns with an episode honoring voices of resistance and conscience; soul and neo‑soul become the musical counterpart to a preacher’s fearless plea.

published.fact_text: In 1982, Guatemala’s government under Fernando Romeo Lucas García was toppled in a military coup led by General Efraín Ríos Montt. The change of power did not bring peace; instead it marked the escalation of a brutal counterinsurgency campaign that would later be described as genocidal against Indigenous Maya communities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A Central American coup and its human cost resonate with an episode about Residente, whose work relentlessly critiques Latin American power structures and violence.

published.fact_text: On 23 March 1983, U.S. President Ronald Reagan announced the Strategic Defense Initiative, a plan to build space‑ and ground‑based systems to intercept incoming nuclear missiles. Nicknamed 'Star Wars', it blended genuine research into missile defense with almost science‑fiction visions of lasers, particle beams, and orbiting battle stations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A futuristic missile shield dubbed 'Star Wars' fits the bright, machine‑driven, cosmic dance energy of an episode about electronic evolution and spacey optimism.
